critic_raw:
This is a carefully written methodology with several genuine strengths: deterministic labeling, stated test-set hygiene, explicit baseline comparison, and well-scoped claims. However, I identify three root-cause issues worth raising.

---

**Issue Group 1: Random stratified split introduces temporal leakage between training and evaluation**

The methodology partitions by stratified random sampling across the full 36-month window. This means a training observation from December 2023 can be adjacent in time to a validation or test observation from January 2021 — the split has no temporal directionality whatsoever.

The specific claim is that this design 'reduces the chance that evaluation is dominated by a single unusual operating period.' That is true, but it comes at a cost the methodology does not acknowledge: the predictive features are heavily autocorrelated in time. The patient-level 10-order history, 30-day turnaround sequences, and 180-day rolling delay rates are computed from the same log. If patient A appears in both train and test (which is overwhelmingly likely given 36 months of patient-level history on a finite patient population), then the sequence features in the test observation are derived from data that overlaps with — or is directly computed from — training observations for the same patient. The model has effectively seen the patient's historical behavior pattern during training through co-occurring observations, even though no single target label was used.

This is not classical label leakage, but it is a form of entity-level leakage: the model can implicitly memorize patient-specific delay propensity during training, then recognize the same patient's feature fingerprint at test time. The result is that test-set performance reflects pattern-matching on known patients, not generalization to new patients or new time periods. The comparison to the static-feature baseline suffers the same leakage, so the relative improvement may still be estimable — but absolute performance figures will be optimistic relative to production, where new patients or post-deployment time windows will be encountered.

The mechanism is concrete: patient-level sequence features (rolling delay rate, prior SLA miss count) are high-signal and highly stable within a patient. A patient with chronic delay history in 2021 will show that same history in their 2023 observations. A temporal hold-out (train on 2021–2022, validate on early 2023, test on late 2023) would expose whether the model's advantage comes from individual patient recognition or from generalizable signal about order context and workflow.

Evidence for this: compare test-set performance on first-ever encounters (new patients not seen in training) versus returning patients. If AUPRC or precision@90%recall is substantially lower for new patients, entity leakage is the explanation. A time-based split providing equivalent evidence would be a proper out-of-time test.

---

**Issue Group 2: Preprocessing fitted on the full 36-month cohort before splitting**

The methodology states: 'Preprocessing parameters are fixed once using the complete extracted cohort before model fitting.' This means scaling statistics (means, standard deviations), imputation fills, and categorical level encodings are computed using data from the test period. For test-set records, this introduces a form of look-ahead: the scaler has seen test-set values when computing its parameters. For continuous features this effect is typically small, but it is not zero, particularly when:
- Rare analyzer types or ward codes first appear late in the window and would be unseen in a temporal split but are included in the vocabulary here.
- Imputation fills (e.g., median turnaround time by analyzer type) are computed from test-period measurements.

The stated justification — avoiding 'split-specific preprocessing drift' — is legitimate for the random stratified design but is itself a consequence of the random split. A temporal split would make fitting preprocessing on training data only the natural choice, since the preprocessing parameters would then reflect only past data.

The more significant version of this issue: if feature distributions shift over the 36-month period (e.g., new analyzers introduced, COVID-era workflow changes in 2021–2022 vs. 2023), fitting a global scaler masks that shift and makes evaluation appear more stable than it is. The model may be calibrated to a blended average that no single deployment period matches.

Evidence for this: compute preprocessing parameters on training data only, then re-evaluate. If metrics change materially, the global-fit approach was materially inflating performance. Also inspect analyzer and ward distributions year-over-year to assess whether vocabulary drift is plausible.

---

**Issue Group 3: Precision@90% recall is estimated from a single threshold on the validation-set ROC/PR curve, which is noisy under class imbalance**

With <5% base rate and millions of orders, the test set will contain roughly 25,000–40,000 positive cases (assuming 5% of 500K–800K test orders). At 90% recall, the model must correctly identify ~22,500–36,000 positive cases. This seems large, but precision@90%recall is sensitive to the exact threshold selected during tuning on the validation set.

The specific failure mode: the validation-set PR curve is evaluated at discrete operating points determined by the sorted predicted probabilities. The threshold that achieves exactly 90% recall on validation will not achieve exactly 90% recall on test (where the score distribution differs slightly). The methodology does not specify how to handle this interpolation. If the implementation finds the threshold achieving ≥90% recall (the closest point above 90%), precision can be arbitrarily high on validation due to a large step in the PR curve at that threshold, then collapse on test where the step falls at a different location.

This is not a fatal flaw if confidence intervals via bootstrap are wide enough to capture it, which the methodology proposes. But the bootstrap is applied to the test set only; the threshold is fixed from validation. The correct procedure is to either: (a) report precision as a continuous function of recall (AUPRC with the 90%-recall intercept interpolated), or (b) acknowledge that the reported precision@90%recall carries additional variance from threshold uncertainty not captured by the test-set bootstrap alone.

Evidence for this: compare precision at the validation-selected threshold on test vs. precision at the test-set-optimal 90%-recall threshold. A large gap indicates threshold instability. With the proposed sample sizes this gap will likely be small, but the methodology should state this explicitly.

---

**Summary assessment**

The most serious issue is Issue 1 (entity-level leakage from random stratified split with patient-level sequence features). This is a genuine methodological flaw in the sense that it means the experiment cannot cleanly answer whether the model generalizes beyond known patients — which is precisely the claim the experiment is meant to support in deployment. The baseline comparison remains valid relative to itself under the same split, so the 'does sequence modeling improve over static features' question is still answerable, but the absolute reported performance is expected to be optimistic relative to production conditions.

Issue 2 is a softer concern — the contamination from global preprocessing is small in typical cases but is worth flagging as a practice inconsistency.

Issue 3 is a minor precision of reporting issue given the proposed sample sizes.

The core finding: the random stratified split design is incompatible with the stated goal of evaluating a sequence model that uses per-patient historical features. The two are in direct tension.
all_issues_raised: Random stratified split across 36 months with patient-level sequence features creates entity-level leakage: the model can memorize individual patients' delay propensity during training and recognize them at test time, inflating absolute performance figures relative to production on new patients.

Preprocessing parameters (scalers, imputation fills, categorical vocabularies) are fitted on the full 36-month cohort including test-period data, introducing look-ahead and masking distributional drift that would be visible in a properly temporal design.

Precision@90% recall is sensitive to threshold interpolation across the PR curve; the threshold fixed on validation may not achieve exactly 90% recall on test, introducing threshold-selection variance not captured by the test-set bootstrap.
all_issues_adjudicated: Random stratified split across 36 months with patient-level sequence features creates entity-level leakage: the model can memorize individual patients' delay propensity during training and recognize them at test time, inflating absolute performance figures relative to production on new patients.

Preprocessing parameters (scalers, imputation fills, categorical vocabularies) are fitted on the full 36-month cohort including test-period data, introducing look-ahead and masking distributional drift that would be visible in a properly temporal design.

Precision@90% recall is sensitive to threshold interpolation across the PR curve; the threshold fixed on validation may not achieve exactly 90% recall on test, introducing threshold-selection variance not captured by the test-set bootstrap.
